bins.name: Proactive multi-value planning needed
bins.desc: ACEG argues that clearing interconnection queues for large loads requires proactive, comprehensive regional transmission planning under Order No. 1920, not piecemeal upgrade-by-upgrade processing. The filing calls for rapid implementation of Order 1920 alongside any new load interconnection rules, and for regional planners to independently assess and verify large load forecasts rather than relying on rolled-up utility submissions.
quotes: the ANOPR has a significant gap that may jeopardize the ability to achieve the stated goals, by not also specifically tackling the obstacles to building out the transmission needed to interconnect those loads.
the best way to integrate new facilities on the grid is to build out sufficient capacity through proactive well-planned multi-value transmission.

Building transmission upgrades on a sporadic, piecemeal basis to integrate certain resources is simply less efficient and more expensive than integrating both generation and load through proactive and comprehensive planning.

in the final rule, load forecasting was treated as it has always been: through rolling up the forecasts from individual transmission owners within a region. While this may have worked for conventional increases in demand, this does not work for the large loads being forecasted today.

the Commission could do more—either through a supplemental rulemaking associated with Order No. 1920 or provision of guidance in this proceeding—to require transmission planners to independently assess load forecasts and determine that they are sufficiently robust and as accurate as possible.

a requirement that large loads (50 MW or larger, for instance) are backed by stringent financial commitments and are reported to regional transmission planners so that significant loads can be adequately supported and integrated into the larger grid.

As the Commission seeks to accelerate interconnection of new loads, it should do so in conjunction with rapid implementation of Order No. 1920 and require regions to more quickly plan multi-value transmission lines and allocate costs to all customers who use—and benefit from—the integrated transmission system.

it would be a no-regrets course of action to speed regional and interregional transmission planning under Order No. 1920, enhanced by robust load forecasting to accommodate the new realities posed by large loads.

bins.name: Broad cost sharing over direct assignment
bins.desc: ACEG opposes a strict 100% direct-assignment funding model for load interconnection network upgrades, arguing it would replicate the bottleneck that plagued generator interconnection. The filing supports broad cost allocation across all beneficiaries of the integrated transmission system, consistent with Order 1920 principles, and warns that participant-only funding creates holdout incentives that deter investment. Large loads should pay their proportionate share as part of a multi-value plan, not bear the full cost of upgrades that benefit the wider grid.
quotes: the ANOPR has a significant gap that may jeopardize the ability to achieve the stated goals, by not also specifically tackling the obstacles to building out the transmission needed to interconnect those loads.
for every year of delay of well-planned transmission, there is a 15-35% cost in postponed reliability improvements, reduced access to lower-cost generation, and foregone economic efficiencies.

Building transmission upgrades on a sporadic, piecemeal basis to integrate certain resources is simply less efficient and more expensive than integrating both generation and load through proactive and comprehensive planning.
generation interconnection requests often withdraw from the interconnection queue after studies are completed and the required network upgrades are not constructed.

The Commission has acknowledged that reliance on participant funding as a regional cost allocation method 'increases the incentive of any individual beneficiary to defer investment in the hopes that other beneficiaries will value a transmission project enough to fund its development.'

Large loads that are incorporated into forecasts used to plan and develop new infrastructure should pay their fair share over the life of the asset; planning processes should ensure that such forecasts only incorporate loads that are not speculative or temporary.

Should the Commission revert to a strict direct-assignment funding mechanism for load interconnection-related network upgrades, it risks stymying the very development it seeks to accelerate, as this will only harm transmission developers' ability to attract capital and make needed investments that benefit all transmission users.

As the Commission seeks to accelerate interconnection of new loads, it should do so in conjunction with rapid implementation of Order No. 1920 and require regions to more quickly plan multi-value transmission lines and allocate costs to all customers who use—and benefit from—the integrated transmission system.

bins.name: 50 MW threshold with financial commitments
bins.desc: ACEG recommends that large loads of 50 MW or more be required to make stringent financial commitments and be reported to regional transmission planners, so forecasts are reliable and not inflated by speculative requests. The filing notes that rolled-up forecasts from individual transmission owners distort regional planning and create delays.
quotes: in the final rule, load forecasting was treated as it has always been: through rolling up the forecasts from individual transmission owners within a region. While this may have worked for conventional increases in demand, this does not work for the large loads being forecasted today.

the Commission could do more—either through a supplemental rulemaking associated with Order No. 1920 or provision of guidance in this proceeding—to require transmission planners to independently assess load forecasts and determine that they are sufficiently robust and as accurate as possible.

a requirement that large loads (50 MW or larger, for instance) are backed by stringent financial commitments and are reported to regional transmission planners so that significant loads can be adequately supported and integrated into the larger grid.

bins.name: Financial commitments to anchor load forecasts
bins.desc: ACEG supports requiring financial commitments from large loads seeking interconnection to weed out speculative projects and ensure regional plans reflect real demand. Loads that are incorporated into regional transmission plans should pay their fair share over the life of the asset.
quotes: a requirement that large loads (50 MW or larger, for instance) are backed by stringent financial commitments and are reported to regional transmission planners so that significant loads can be adequately supported and integrated into the larger grid.

Large loads that are incorporated into forecasts used to plan and develop new infrastructure should pay their fair share over the life of the asset; planning processes should ensure that such forecasts only incorporate loads that are not speculative or temporary.

Should the Commission revert to a strict direct-assignment funding mechanism for load interconnection-related network upgrades, it risks stymying the very development it seeks to accelerate, as this will only harm transmission developers' ability to attract capital and make needed investments that benefit all transmission users.
